reasoning: To achieve a $250 billion nominal decrease in federal expenditures (FGEXPND) from Q4 2024 to Q4 2025, the government would have to overcome the natural baseline growth of spending. Mandatory spending (Social Security, Medicare) and interest on the debt automatically drive nominal spending up by hundreds of billions annually. Because President-elect Trump promised to protect Social Security and Medicare, and defense spending is unlikely to be severely cut, any reductions must fall on non-defense discretionary spending and subsidies. Non-defense discretionary spending is less than $1 trillion in total. Slashing this category by enough to offset automatic baseline growth and force an absolute $250 billion year-over-year decline is politically and practically extreme. Even with the appointment of Elon Musk to the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE), such massive cuts in the first year are highly restricted by legislative friction, political inertia, and the Impoundment Control Act. Accordingly, a nominal reduction of this magnitude is highly improbable.
